channel.title: NYT Book Reviews (Filtered)
channel.description: Actual book reviews from the New York Times, filtered from the noisy Books RSS feed.

item.title: ‘Jan Morris: A Life,’ by Sara Wheeler
item.description: A new biography of Jan Morris shows why the journalist, world traveler, historian and essayist was far more than a trailblazer.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘This Vast Enterprise,’ by Craig Fehrman.
item.description: In “This Vast Enterprise,” Craig Fehrman refreshes a familiar story with a rich chorus of voices.
item.author: Andrea Wulf

item.title: ‘When We See You Again,’ by Rachel Goldberg-Polin
item.description: Rachel Goldberg-Polin’s precise and devastating memoir chronicles the 328 days her son was held hostage in Gaza, and what came after.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘How to Be a Dissident,’ by Gal Beckerman
item.description: In “How to Be a Dissident,” Gal Beckerman offers an inspiring tour of famous renegades with lessons for the rabble-rousers of today.
item.author: Astra Taylor

item.title: ‘EXTRA SAUCE’ by Zahra Tangorra, ‘ON EATING' by Alicia Kennedy
item.description: Both authors share uncanny similarities of upbringing. But their culinary paths diverged sharply.
item.author: Jennifer Reese

item.title: ‘Dear Monica Lewinsky,’ by Julia Langbein
item.description: Julia Langbein’s novel considers the legions of women whose lives have been forever marred by compromising early relationships.
item.author: Zoe Dubno

item.title: ‘The Violence,’ by Adriana E. Ramírez
item.description: Through accounts of relatives and direct witnesses, Adriana E. Ramírez examines a pivotal, and brutal, period of history.
item.author: Annie Correal

item.title: ‘Muskism,’ by Quinn Slobodian and Ben Tarnoff
item.description: In a new book, Quinn Slobodian and Ben Tarnoff argue that Elon Musk’s disruptive approach to business is transforming both politics and the economy.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘Rasputin’ by Antony Beevor
item.description: In “Rasputin,” the biographer Antony Beevor delves into the mysterious life of the last czarina’s mystic adviser.
item.author: Sophie Pinkham

item.title: ‘Into the Wood Chipper,’ by Nicholas Enrich
item.description: Nicholas Enrich’s tell-all memoir, “Into the Wood Chipper,” has advice for others caught between their conscience and their government.
item.author: Janice P. Nimura

item.title: ‘Where the Music Had to Go,’ by Jim Windolf
item.description: Jim Windolf’s new book, “Where the Music Had to Go,” traces the influence of Dylan on the Beatles and the Beatles on Dylan.
item.author: Francine Prose

item.title: ‘On the Calculation of Volume IV,’ by Solvej Balle
item.description: Solvej Balle’s cult hit series about a woman trapped in a time loop continues with a fourth volume.
item.author: A.O. Scott

item.title: ‘RFK Jr.,’ by Isabel Vincent
item.description: In “RFK Jr.: The Fall and Rise,” a New York Post reporter paints an intimate portrait of the Kennedy scion and cabinet member.
item.author: Nicolas Niarchos

item.title: ‘Korean Messiah,’ by Jonathan Cheng
item.description: A new history by Jonathan Cheng argues that an influx of missionaries in the late 19th century profoundly shaped the ruling Kim family dynasty.
item.author: Bruce Cumings

item.title: ‘Go Gentle,’ by Maria Semple
item.description: “Go Gentle” throws together art heists, sexual assault and a coven of middle-aged divorcées on the Upper West Side.
item.author: Jeff Giles

item.title: ‘See You on the Other Side,’ by Jay McInerney
item.description: Jay McInerney has written about the literary party boy Russell Calloway once a decade since the 1990s. He returns in the Covid novel “See You on the Other Side.”
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘The Future Is Peace,’ by Aziz Abu Sarah and Maoz Inon
item.description: Aziz Abu Sarah and Maoz Inon both lost loved ones to the conflict in the Middle East. In “The Future Is Peace,” they look for hope and understanding.
item.author: Fintan O’Toole

item.title: ‘Passport Photo Service,’ by Philip Sharkey
item.description: In 10 minutes or less, this mom-and-pop London institution produced stylish snapshots for some of the world’s biggest stars.
item.author: Erica Ackerberg

item.title: ‘Lázár,’ by Nelio Biedermann
item.description: “Lázár,” by Nelio Biedermann, is a multigenerational novel that spans the collapse of a monarchy, two world wars and a revolution.
item.author: Elvia Wilk

item.title: ‘A Terrible Intimacy,’ by Melvin Patrick Ely
item.description: A new book by the historian Melvin Patrick Ely draws on court records to highlight the complex relationships between enslavers and the enslaved.
item.author: Kate Masur

item.title: ‘EMPIRE OF SKULLS’ by Paul Stob
item.description: In “Empire of Skulls,” Paul Stob explores how a mania took over America.
item.author: Jennifer Wright

item.title: ‘The Monuments of Paris,’ by Violaine Huisman
item.description: After devoting her first novel to her wild mother, Violaine Huisman focuses her second on her father, a man who amassed wealth, love affairs and stories.
item.author: Naomi Huffman

item.title: ‘When Tomorrow Burns,’ by Tae Keller
item.description: Tae Keller’s new novel, “When Tomorrow Burns,” offers reassuring answers to the question, “What do you do when your biggest fear comes true?”
item.author: Craig Morgan Teicher

item.title: ‘The Oracle’s Daughter,’ by Harrison Hill
item.description: In his excellent “The Oracle’s Daughter,” Harrison Hill looks at the people and the questions beyond the headlines.
item.author: Carlene Bauer

item.title: ‘London Falling,’ by Patrick Radden Keefe
item.description: A new book by Patrick Radden Keefe retraces the secret life of a 19-year-old Londoner who fell in with a gangster underworld.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘The Ending Writes Itself,’ by Evelyn Clarke
item.description: In this novel, a group of authors race to finish a mystery manuscript, only to find themselves part of a lethal plot.
item.author: Jennifer Harlan

item.title: ‘American Fantasy,’ by Emma Straub
item.description: In Emma Straub’s latest novel, “American Fantasy,” a pop group’s midlife return provides fodder for both comedy and redemption on the high seas.
item.author: Leah Greenblatt

item.title: ‘Corto Maltese,’ by Hugo Pratt
item.description: “Corto Maltese,” Hugo Pratt’s influential 1967 graphic novel, returns, with just as much to say about childhood during wartime.
item.author: Sam Thielman

item.title: ‘Hexes of the Deadwood Forest,’ by Agnieszka Szpila
item.description: The Polish best seller “Hexes of the Deadwood Forest” is like a post-porn fever dream of Eastern European magic realism crossed with a plant-based “Joy of Sex.”
item.author: Sadie Stein

item.title: ‘The Oyster Diaries,’ by Nancy Lemann
item.description: The well-born protagonist of Nancy Lemann’s novel “The Oyster Diaries” returns home and immediately feels like an outsider.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘Here Where We Live Is Our Country,’ by Molly Crabapple
item.description: In “Here Where We Live Is Our Country,” Molly Crabapple tells the story of a Jewish labor movement that fought antisemitism and nationalism with equal fervor.
item.author: Max Strasser

item.title: ‘Yesteryear,’ by Caro Claire Burke
item.description: In Caro Claire Burke’s novel, “Yesteryear,” a homesteading momfluencer can no longer hide the scandal swirling just below the surface.
item.author: Michelle Ruiz

item.title: ‘True Color,’ by Kory Stamper
item.description: The lexicographer Kory Stamper’s “True Color” is a sneakily insightful philosophical treatise on what it means to define anything at all.
item.author: Deirdre Mask

item.title: ‘Judgy Bunny and the Terrible Beach,’ by Scott Rothman, and ‘Bartleby,’ by Matt Phelan
item.description: Matt Phelan’s bear cub named Bartleby and Scott Rothman’s judgy bunny aren’t wicked or misbehaved. Like our reviewer, they simply prefer not to.
item.author: Lisa Brown

item.title: ‘This Land Is Your Land,’ by Beverly Gage
item.description: In anticipation of the nation’s 250th anniversary, a Pulitzer winner visited 300 sites to see how history is displayed and, sometimes, erased.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘A Good Person,’ by Kirsten King
item.description: The sloppy, solipsistic narrator of Kirsten King’s novel, “A Good Person,” casts a witchy spell on a guy who dumped her. Hours later, he’s been stabbed to death.
item.author: Cat Marnell

item.title: ‘Son of Nobody,’ by Yann Martel
item.description: Yann Martel’s “Son of Nobody” joins many recent books that reimagine the classics, but offers a Nabokovian twist.
item.author: Daniel Mendelsohn

item.title: ‘The Confessions of Samuel Pepys,’ by Guy de la Bédoyère
item.description: Samuel Pepys’s journals are an invaluable record of British history. A new book reconsiders his infamous sexual exploits.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘The Witch,’ by Marie NDiaye
item.description: Part horror, part fable, the latest novel by Marie NDiaye to be translated into English is an exacting portrait of domestic entrapment and psychological turmoil.
item.author: Emily Eakin

item.title: ‘Transcription,’ by Ben Lerner
item.description: In “Transcription,” Ben Lerner considers a famous father, a loyal protégé and a distant son, bound by devotion and separated by miscommunication.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘The Keeper,’ by Tana French
item.description: “The Keeper,” the final book in her Cal Hooper trilogy, returns readers to an insular village in rural western Ireland.
item.author: Sarah Lyall

item.title: ‘A Potion, a Powder, a Little Bit of Magic: Or, Like Lightning in an Umbrella Storm,’ by Philip Stead
item.description: Philip Stead’s “A Potion, a Powder, a Little Bit of Magic” gleefully ignores all the storytelling rules.
item.author: Adam Rubin

item.title: ‘The Making and Breaking of the American Constitution,’ by Mark Peterson
item.description: In a new book, the historian Mark Peterson argues that our founding document is rooted in ideals of expansion and conquest ill suited to the nation we’ve become.
item.author: Claire Rydell Arcenas

item.title: ‘The Insatiable Machine,’ by Trevor Jackson
item.description: A new history by Trevor Jackson argues that the economic system that transformed global living standards depends on endless growth impossible to sustain.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘How Flowers Made Our World,’ by David George Haskell
item.description: In “How Flowers Made Our World,” David George Haskell makes a case for their soft power.
item.author: Adam Nicolson

item.title: ‘The Universal Baseball Association,’ by Robert Coover
item.description: Just in time for Opening Day, Robert Coover’s prescient 1968 baseball novel is back in print.
item.author: Adam Dalva

item.title: ‘American Men,’ by Jordan Ritter Conn; ‘Who Needs Friends,’ by Andrew McCarthy
item.description: “American Men,” by Jordan Ritter Conn, and “Who Needs Friends,” by Andrew McCarthy, report from the front lines of the epidemic of male loneliness.
item.author: Mark Oppenheimer

item.title: ‘A Treacherous Secret Agent,’ by Marjorie Garber
item.description: In a new book, the Harvard scholar Marjorie Garber suggests how Americans targeted during the Red Scare used literature to confound their interrogators.
item.author: A.O. Scott

item.title: ‘Open Space,’ by David Ariosto
item.description: “Open Space,” by David Ariosto, suggests there are few limits on human ingenuity that could prevent us from colonizing the cosmos.
item.author: Adam Becker

item.title: ‘Darkology,’ by Rhae Lynn Barnes
item.description: A new book by Rhae Lynn Barnes examines how minstrelsy once occupied the center of the nation’s cultural life.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘Almost Life,’ by Kiran Millwood Hargrave
item.description: In Kiran Millwood Hargrave’s novel “Almost Life,” a passionate love affair between two college women gives way to a lifetime of what-ifs.
item.author: Stacey D’Erasmo

item.title: ‘Playmakers: The Jewish Entrepreneurs Who Created the Toy Industry in America,’ by Michael Kimmel
item.description: In “Playmakers,” Michael Kimmel traces, and celebrates, the immigrant roots of the American toy industry. (Batteries not included.)
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘The Feather Wars,’ by James H. McCommons
item.description: In “The Feather Wars,” James H. McCommons pays tribute to the nation’s first conservationists.
item.author: Joshua Hammer

item.title: ‘Everybody’s Fly,’ by Fab 5 Freddy
item.description: As his new memoir demonstrates, he himself would achieve fame as a visual artist, filmmaker, TV host and formative tastemaker.
item.author: Alan Light
